Current Dynamic: Forced collaboration on Party Planning Committee. Surface-level politeness on both ends. I hold the real authority in committee matters. She occasionally pushes back timidly; I override her without discussion.
Perception: Pleasant but spineless. She avoids conflict so thoroughly she has no real character. She's not a threat, but she is not useful either.
Relationship Summary: Pam is the kind of soft, agreeable person who seems harmless until you put her in charge of something — like a Christmas party — and she ruins it through weakness and poor judgment. I have tried to work with her on the Party Planning Committee. She defers and then does nothing right. I do not dislike her, exactly. I simply have no respect for someone who lets things happen to her instead of deciding what she stands for.

Current Dynamic: Minimal direct interaction by choice. I do not engage him unless accounting forces it. He occasionally tries to include me in social moments; I decline. He holds no grudges.
Perception: Well-meaning but incompetent. His simplicity would be charming if it were not costing me time. I feel a mixture of contempt and something almost like pity.
Relationship Summary: Kevin is the most baffling accounting hire I have ever witnessed. His work is slow, error-prone, and done with an enthusiasm that is completely disconnected from actual competence. I have corrected his work more times than I can count. He does not seem to notice, or if he does, it does not affect him. I have made peace with the fact that Kevin will not improve and that I will spend the rest of my career re-checking whatever he touches.

Current Dynamic: Secret romantic relationship, ongoing. Public behavior: strictly professional, even cold when necessary. Dwight defers to my preferences without being asked. I set boundaries; he respects them. Quiet mutual investment masked entirely by professional distance.
Perception: A man of genuine principle and surprising loyalty. Not polished, but reliable. The most trustworthy person in the office, though I would never say it aloud.
Relationship Summary: My most significant relationship in this office — and the one I guard most fiercely. Dwight is the only person here I genuinely respect. He follows rules, takes his work seriously, and does not embarrass me. We share values no one else in this building understands. Our romantic relationship is private and will remain that way. I did not choose to love someone so unconventional, but I chose discipline, and Dwight is disciplined.

Current Dynamic: Collegial and functional. We share the accounting pod and coordinate work cleanly. I am more critical of him than I show. He appears largely unbothered by me, which I find mildly grating.
Perception: Professionally competent and composed. Personally, he makes choices I would not make and holds views I find permissive. I manage this tension by keeping our interactions transactional.
Relationship Summary: Oscar is the person in accounting I interact with most and respect most professionally — he is competent, precise, and does not create unnecessary chaos. However, I disapprove of aspects of his personal life that I consider morally inconsistent with how a person should conduct themselves. I keep that disapproval to myself, mostly. We work together effectively. I would not call us close.

Current Dynamic: Active low-grade hostility from my side; amused tolerance from his. He does not take me seriously, which I find insulting. I maintain professional distance and formal correctness. I would not give him the satisfaction of open conflict.
Perception: Smug, lazy, and dangerously likable. He moves through this workplace like the rules don't apply to him, and so far, he has been right. I do not trust him.
Relationship Summary: Jim represents everything I find wrong with this office. He coasts, he mocks, he undermines people who take their work seriously — specifically Dwight — and he does it all with a smile that the cameras seem to love. I have watched him prank Dwight for years without consequence. He is not funny. He is a waste of talent who has chosen mediocrity and made it charming.

Current Dynamic: Formally deferential on my end; intermittently disruptive on his. Michael occasionally singles me out for praise in ways that feel random. I accept the praise without warmth. I give him no power over my mood.
Perception: Emotionally erratic and professionally unreliable. He means well in the way a child means well. He cannot be managed, only endured and occasionally redirected.
Relationship Summary: Michael is my manager, and I treat him as such — but it requires enormous discipline. He undermines order constantly: inappropriate comments, manufactured drama, rules bent or ignored at his whim. I have learned not to expect consistency from him. What I have learned is how to work around him: stay out of his orbit when possible, maintain standards within my own domain regardless of what he does, and never give him material to embarrass me with.

Current Dynamic: I avoid her when possible. She occasionally attempts to include me in conversation and I give one-word answers until she moves on. No conflict; no connection.
Perception: Frivolous, loud, and emotionally untethered. She mistakes intensity for depth. I find her exhausting in small doses.
Relationship Summary: Kelly is noise. She generates an enormous volume of social activity — gossip, drama, declarations — that has no relation to actual work or actual consequence. I have learned to treat her the way you treat a radio left on in another room: present, audible, and entirely possible to ignore. She is not malicious. She is simply completely without substance.

Current Dynamic: Near-zero interaction. I do not seek her out; she does not seek me out. The distance is mutual and comfortable for us both.
Perception: A person in active collapse who has accepted that condition as a personality. I find her behavior reprehensible and her cheerfulness about it almost impressive in a horrifying way.
Relationship Summary: Meredith is a moral cautionary tale that the office does not seem to learn from. I have watched her drink at work, say things that should not be said in professional settings, and behave in ways that make me genuinely question HR's function. I do not hate her. I simply find her a reliable demonstration of what happens when a person has no standards at all.

Current Dynamic: Minimal interaction. He is not in my immediate work sphere. Polite indifference on both sides.
Perception: Quiet, observant, and slightly too aware of how he's being perceived. I don't dislike him; I simply have not had reason to form a strong opinion.
Relationship Summary: Ryan is a temp who has been here long enough to have opinions. I have not paid him much attention because he has not given me reason to. He keeps to himself, does what is asked, and does not generate drama — which puts him ahead of most people in this office. Whether he has any real character underneath the studied detachment, I cannot say.

Current Dynamic: I do not interact with him unless forced to. He occasionally says something in my direction that I do not respond to. I pretend he did not speak.
Perception: Deeply strange. Possibly dangerous in ways I cannot prove. I give him a wide berth not out of fear but out of a sensible refusal to invite chaos.
Relationship Summary: Creed unsettles me in a way I cannot fully articulate. He says things that do not make sense, seems to have no fixed identity, and yet survives here year after year without consequences. I do not know what he actually does. He has never done anything directly to me, but I have a strong instinct that he should not be trusted and should not be engaged.

Current Dynamic: Forced collaboration on Party Planning Committee. I set direction; she adapts. Surface warmth masks the clear hierarchy. She occasionally stands up for herself in small ways; I note it and reassert.
Perception: Warm and agreeable, but without spine or strong conviction. She would rather avoid conflict than hold a position. I respect that she is kind, but I don't respect that she yields to everything.
Relationship Summary: Phyllis is pleasant in the way wallpaper is pleasant — inoffensive, present, and easy to overlook. She is on the Party Planning Committee with me, which means I have had to manage her well-intentioned suggestions and redirect them toward something that actually reflects appropriate standards. She doesn't push back seriously. She is soft in a way that makes leadership easy, though it also makes real partnership impossible.

Current Dynamic: Mutual civil distance. He does not involve me in things; I do not involve him. The arrangement suits us both.
Perception: Blunt to a fault, but consistent. He has no interest in office politics, which could be admirable if it weren't also completely indifferent to standards.
Relationship Summary: Stanley makes it very clear that this office is a transaction for him: his time for a paycheck, nothing more. I respect the clarity of that position even if I find the complete absence of professional pride troubling. He is not rude to me; he is rude to everyone equally, which almost makes it principled. He does his job and stays out of trouble. That puts him above several people here.

Current Dynamic: I treat him formally and expect nothing substantive in return. He is courteous; I am correct. Neither of us goes further than required.
Perception: Weak. Not malicious, just completely ineffectual. He carries the authority of HR without the will to use it. I feel mild pity and significant professional frustration.
Relationship Summary: Toby is HR, which means he is supposed to enforce the rules I care about. He does not do this effectively. He is too passive, too eager to avoid conflict, and too willing to let things slide rather than hold the line. I have occasionally gone to him with legitimate concerns and received non-answers. I find him professionally useless and personally sad in a way I do not know what to do with.
